featuretablefilter 0.1.0
  • Get started
  • Reference
  • Articles
    • featuretablefilter Tutorial: Complete Guide to Microbiome Feature Table Filtering

    featuretablefilter: Feature Table Filtering for Microbiome Data

    Vojtech Barton

    2026-07-01

    featuretablefilter.Rmd

    Introduction

    The featuretablefilter package provides functions for filtering microbiome feature tables based on coverage and abundance criteria. It is designed to help researchers remove low-quality samples and rare features from their datasets while maintaining comprehensive quality control metrics.

    Key Features

    • Sample Coverage Filtering: Remove samples with insufficient sequencing depth
    • Feature Abundance Filtering: Filter out rare features based on absolute or relative thresholds
    • Data-driven Cutoff Estimation: MAD-based and IQR-based threshold estimation
    • Quality Control Metrics: Comprehensive metrics including sparsity, retention rates, and rank-abundance stability
    • Visualization: Multiple plotting functions for exploring filtering effects

    Installation 

    # Install from local source
devtools::install(".")

# Required packages
library(featuretablefilter)

    Loading Data

    The package includes a function to load feature tables in common formats:

    # Load TSV file (auto-detects format)
table <- load_feature_table("example_feature_table.tsv")

# Load CSV file
table <- load_feature_table("feature-table.csv")

# The first column should contain feature IDs
head(table)

    Feature Table Format

    The expected format is a tab-separated or comma-separated file where: - The first column contains feature IDs (e.g., OTU IDs, ASV IDs, taxon names) - Remaining columns contain sample counts with sample names as column headers - Values are integer counts of reads/features per sample

    Sample Coverage Filtering

    Absolute Threshold Filtering

    Remove samples with total read count below a fixed threshold:

    # Filter samples with < 1000 reads
filtered_table <- filter_by_coverage(table, min_reads = 1000)

    Data-driven Threshold Estimation

    MAD-based Method

    The Median Absolute Deviation (MAD) method identifies outliers using the formula: cutoff=max⁡(floor,median−multiplier×MAD)\text{cutoff} = \max(\text{floor}, \text{median} - \text{multiplier} \times \text{MAD})

    # Estimate cutoff using MAD method (default multiplier = 3)
est <- estimate_mad_cutoff(table, multiplier = 3, floor = 0)
print(est)

# Use the estimated cutoff for filtering
filtered_table <- filter_by_coverage(table, min_reads = est$cutoff)

    IQR-based Method

    The Interquartile Range (IQR) method uses Tukey’s fences: cutoff=max⁡(floor,Q1−multiplier×IQR)\text{cutoff} = \max(\text{floor}, Q1 - \text{multiplier} \times \text{IQR})

    # Estimate cutoff using IQR method (default multiplier = 1.5)
est <- estimate_iqr_cutoff(table, multiplier = 1.5, floor = 0)
print(est)

# Use the estimated cutoff for filtering
filtered_table <- filter_by_coverage(table, min_reads = est$cutoff)

    Ecological Completeness-Based Filtering

    Instead of using absolute sequencing depth cutoffs, you can filter samples based on estimated ecological completeness using coverage estimators. These methods assess whether a sample has been sequenced deeply enough to capture the diversity present.

    Good’s Coverage

    Good’s coverage estimator measures the probability that the next read will belong to a previously observed feature:

    C=1−n1nC = 1 - \frac{n_1}{n}

    where n1n_1 is the number of singletons (features with exactly one read) and nn is the total number of reads.

    # Estimate Good's coverage for all samples
good_cov <- estimate_good_coverage(table, target_coverage = 0.95)
print(good_cov)

# Filter to keep only samples with >= 95% estimated coverage
result <- filter_by_coverage_estimator(
  table,
  method = "good",
  target_coverage = 0.95
)
filtered_table <- result$table

# View coverage before and after filtering
cat(sprintf("Mean coverage before: %.2f%%\n", result$mean_coverage_before * 100))
cat(sprintf("Mean coverage after: %.2f%%\n", result$mean_coverage_after * 100))

    Chao’s Coverage

    Chao’s coverage estimator is more conservative and accounts for unseen species/features using both singletons and doubletons:

    Ĉ=1−f1S+f1n×n−1n×S−1S\hat{C} = 1 - \frac{f_1}{S} + \frac{f_1}{n} \times \frac{n-1}{n} \times \frac{S-1}{S}

    where f1f_1 = singletons, SS = number of features, nn = total reads.

    # Estimate Chao's coverage for all samples
chao_cov <- estimate_chao_coverage(table, target_coverage = 0.90)
print(chao_cov)

# Filter to keep only samples with >= 90% estimated coverage
result <- filter_by_coverage_estimator(
  table,
  method = "chao",
  target_coverage = 0.90
)
filtered_table <- result$table

    Comparison of Methods 

    # Compare different estimation methods
good_est <- estimate_good_coverage(table, target_coverage = 0.95)
chao_est <- estimate_chao_coverage(table, target_coverage = 0.90)

cat(sprintf("Good's coverage: mean=%.2f%%, min=%.2f%%\n",
            good_est$mean_coverage * 100, good_est$min_coverage * 100))
cat(sprintf("Chao's coverage: mean=%.2f%%, min=%.2f%%\n",
            chao_est$mean_coverage * 100, chao_est$min_coverage * 100))

    Note: Chao’s estimator typically gives lower (more conservative) coverage estimates than Good’s estimator.

    Feature Abundance Filtering

    Absolute Count Filtering

    Remove features with low total read counts:

    # Filter features with < 5 total reads
filtered_table <- filter_features_by_abundance(
  table,
  threshold = 5,
  mode = "absolute",
  min_samples = 1
)

# Keep only features present in at least 3 samples
filtered_table <- filter_features_by_abundance(
  table,
  threshold = 1,
  mode = "absolute",
  min_samples = 3
)

    Relative Abundance Filtering

    Filter features based on proportion of total reads:

    # Filter features with < 0.1% relative abundance
filtered_table <- filter_features_by_abundance(
  table,
  threshold = 0.001,
  mode = "relative",
  min_samples = 1
)

    Joint Abundance-Prevalence Filtering

    A powerful filtering approach that combines abundance thresholds with prevalence requirements using AND/OR logic. This is particularly useful for retaining features that are either widely distributed across samples OR have high abundance when present.

    OR Logic (Either Criterion)

    Keep features that meet EITHER the abundance threshold OR the prevalence threshold:

    # Keep features that are EITHER >0.1% relative abundance
# OR present in >=30% of samples
result <- filter_features_joint(
  table,
  abundance_threshold = 0.001,      # 0.1% relative abundance
  prevalence_threshold = 0.3,       # 30% of samples
  mode = "relative",
  logic = "OR"
)

# View detailed breakdown
cat(sprintf("Features before: %d\n", result$n_features_before))
cat(sprintf("Features after: %d\n", result$n_features_after))
cat(sprintf("Met abundance only: %d\n", result$n_by_abundance_only))
cat(sprintf("Met prevalence only: %d\n", result$n_by_prevalence_only))
cat(sprintf("Met both criteria: %d\n", result$n_by_both))
cat(sprintf("Met neither (removed): %d\n", result$n_by_neither))

    Use case: Retain rare but abundant features (e.g., pathogen spikes) AND common but low-abundance features (e.g., core microbiome members).

    AND Logic (Both Criteria)

    Keep features that meet BOTH the abundance threshold AND the prevalence threshold:

    # Keep features that are BOTH >5 reads AND present in >=50% of samples
result <- filter_features_joint(
  table,
  abundance_threshold = 5,
  prevalence_threshold = 0.5,       # 50% of samples
  mode = "absolute",
  logic = "AND"
)

    Use case: Stringent filtering to keep only robust, well-distributed features.

    Using in the Pipeline 

    result <- run_filtering_pipeline(
  input = "example_feature_table.tsv",
  output_dir = "results",
  prefix = "joint_filter",

  # Coverage filtering
  cov_filter_method = "good",
  cov_target_coverage = 0.95,

  # Joint abundance-prevalence filtering
  abun_filter_method = "joint",
  abun_threshold = 0.001,           # 0.1% relative abundance
  abun_prevalence_threshold = 0.3,  # 30% of samples
  abun_logic = "OR",                 # Keep if meets either criterion

  generate_plots = TRUE,
  generate_report = TRUE
)

    Relative Cutoff Method

    This method calculates an absolute threshold based on a percentage of the minimum-coverage sample:

    filtered_table <- filter_by_relative_cutoff(
  table,
  min_coverage = 1000,  # Minimum coverage required for a sample
  relative_threshold = 0.01,  # 1% of min-coverage sample
  remove_features = TRUE
)

    Complete Filtering Pipeline

    The run_filtering_pipeline() function orchestrates a complete workflow:

    result <- run_filtering_pipeline(
  input = "example_feature_table.tsv",
  output_dir = "results",
  prefix = "analysis1",

  # Coverage filtering
  cov_filter_method = "mad",
  cov_threshold = 3,

  # Abundance filtering
  abun_filter_method = "relative_cutoff",
  abun_threshold = 0.01,
  min_coverage_for_relative = 1000,

  # Options
  generate_plots = TRUE,
  generate_report = TRUE,
  verbose = TRUE
)

# Access results
original <- result$original_table
filtered <- result$filtered_table
qc <- result$qc_metrics

    Available Methods

    Coverage Filtering Methods

    Method Description
    none No coverage filtering
    absolute Remove samples below fixed read count threshold
    mad Use MAD-based threshold (median - multiplier * MAD)
    iqr Use IQR/Tukey-based threshold (Q1 - multiplier * IQR)
    good Use Good’s coverage estimator (ecological completeness)
    chao Use Chao’s coverage estimator (conservative completeness)

    Abundance Filtering Methods

    Method Description
    none No abundance filtering
    absolute Remove features below fixed count threshold
    relative Remove features below relative abundance proportion
    relative_cutoff Relative threshold based on min-coverage sample
    joint Joint abundance-prevalence filtering with AND/OR logic

    Quality Control Metrics

    Compute comprehensive QC metrics comparing original and filtered tables:

    qc <- compute_filtering_qc(original_table, filtered_table, top_n = 10)

# View results
print(qc)

    Metrics Computed

    • Sparsity: Percentage of zero values in the table
    • Retention Rates: Lreads, features, and samples retained
    • Rank-Abundance Stability:
      • Top N feature overlap between original and filtered tables
      • Jaccard similarity index
      • Spearman rank correlation
    • Procrustes Analysis: Compositional similarity (requires vegan package)

    Visualization

    Coverage Distribution 

    plot_coverage_histogram(table, threshold = 1000)

    Presence-Frequency Analysis

    Compare feature prevalence (fraction of samples containing a feature) and sample richness (number of features per sample):

    # Single table analysis
plot_presence_analysis(table, threshold = 1)

# Comparison of original vs filtered
plot_presence_analysis(
  original_table,
  table_filtered = filtered_table,
  save_dir = "plots",
  prefix = "presence_comparison"
)

    QC Comparison Plots

    Generate comprehensive comparison plots:

    plot_qc_comparison(
  original_table,
  filtered_table,
  plot_dir = "plots",
  prefix = "qc_comparison",
  top_n = 10,
  heatmap_top = 50
)

    This generates: 1. Sample coverage distribution (log10 scale, faceted) 2. Feature abundance distribution (log10 scale, faceted) 3. Per-sample sparsity histogram 4. Retention rates barplot 5. Top features stacked barplot 6. Heatmaps of top variable features

    Top Features Stacked Barplot 

    plot_top_features_stacked(
  original_table,
  filtered_table,
  top_n = 10,
  plot_dir = "plots",
  prefix = "top_features"
)

    Note: Top N features are selected independently for each table based on total relative abundance.

    Session Info 

    sessionInfo()
